title: Quiz: Security Models
description: Test your understanding of formal security definitions and cryptographic proofs

questions.question: What does IND-CPA security guarantee?

options.text: Ciphertexts cannot be decrypted without the key
An adversary with encryption access cannot distinguish between encryptions of two chosen messages
The encryption scheme is perfectly secure
Keys cannot be recovered from ciphertexts

questions.explanation: IND-CPA (Indistinguishability under Chosen-Plaintext Attack) means an adversary with access to an encryption oracle cannot tell which of two chosen messages was encrypted with probability significantly better than 1/2.

questions.question: What additional capability does a CCA adversary have compared to a CPA adversary?

options.text: Access to the secret key
Access to a decryption oracle
Unlimited computational power
Knowledge of the random coins used in encryption

questions.explanation: A CCA (Chosen-Ciphertext Attack) adversary can query a decryption oracle for any ciphertext except the challenge ciphertext. This is a stronger attack model than CPA.

questions.question: What does EUF-CMA security mean for a signature scheme?

options.text: Signatures cannot be verified without the public key
Even with access to a signing oracle, an adversary cannot forge a signature on a new message
Signatures are always the same size
Private keys cannot be derived from signatures

questions.explanation: EUF-CMA (Existential Unforgeability under Chosen-Message Attack) means that even an adversary who can request signatures on any chosen messages cannot produce a valid signature on any message they didn't already have signed.

questions.question: What is the key difference between computational and information-theoretic security?

options.text: Computational security is faster
Information-theoretic security holds against unbounded adversaries
Computational security requires longer keys
Information-theoretic security only applies to hash functions

questions.explanation: Information-theoretic security holds even against adversaries with unlimited computational resources. Computational security only holds against bounded (polynomial-time) adversaries and relies on problem hardness assumptions.

questions.question: What is a security reduction in cryptography?

options.text: Making a scheme use smaller keys
A proof that breaking a scheme would solve a believed-hard problem
Simplifying a complex scheme
Reducing the number of rounds in a cipher

questions.explanation: A security reduction proves that if an adversary can break scheme A, they can also solve problem B. Since B is believed hard (like factoring), this provides confidence that A is secure.

questions.question: Textbook RSA (without padding) is IND-CPA secure because RSA is based on the hard factoring problem.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False! Textbook RSA is deterministic—encrypting the same message twice gives the same ciphertext. This violates IND-CPA security because an adversary can easily distinguish encryptions. Padding schemes like OAEP are required for IND-CPA security.

questions.question: Which NIST security level provides 128-bit post-quantum security?

options.text: Level 1
Level 2
Level 3
Level 5

questions.explanation: NIST Level 3 provides 128-bit post-quantum security (equivalent to breaking AES-192). Level 1 provides only 64-bit post-quantum security due to Grover's algorithm.

questions.question: Which of the following are TRUE about the one-time pad? (Select all that apply)

options.text: It provides information-theoretic security
The key must be as long as the message
It is practical for most applications
It cannot be broken even with unlimited computation

questions.explanation: The one-time pad provides perfect (information-theoretic) secrecy—it cannot be broken even with unlimited computation. However, it requires a key as long as the message and perfect randomness, making it impractical for most applications.
